title: Nonlinear Magnon Magnetic Moment Transport in Triangular-Lattice f-Wave Antialtermagnets
authors: Volodymyr P. Kravchuk, Kostiantyn V. Yershov, Bastián Pradenas, Robin R. Neumann, Rodrigo Jaeschke-Ubiergo, Ricardo Zarzuela, Jairo Sinova, Jeroen van den Brink, Alexander Mook
abstract: We study the spin excitations in the frustrated coplanar 120-degree ground state of the triangular-lattice Heisenberg antiferromagnet and demonstrate that they carry a magnetic moment perpendicular to the plane in which the spins order, despite the ground-state sublattice moments having no out-of-plane component. The symmetry of the momentum dependence of the magnetic moment and energy of the magnons renders the system an odd-parity f-wave magnet. Extending this model to a stack of antiferromagnetically coupled triangular layers provides a realization of magnons in a three-dimensional f-wave antialtermagnet. We show that nonlinear thermal transport effects of magnons, such as Edelstein and spin-splitter effects, provide clear experimental signatures of magnons in f-wave antialtermagnets.
summary: This paper identifies a new class of f-wave antialtermagnets where magnons carry a magnetic moment perpendicular to the spin plane. It predicts that these systems will exhibit unique nonlinear thermal transport phenomena, such as the spin-splitter and Edelstein effects, which could be detected via Lorentz microscopy.
main_problem: The study investigates spin excitations (magnons) in a triangular-lattice Heisenberg antiferromagnet to demonstrate that they carry a magnetic moment perpendicular to the spin-ordering plane, identifying the system as an f-wave antialtermagnet.
main_result: The researchers show that magnons in this system possess an out-of-plane magnetic moment and predict nonlinear thermal transport signatures, specifically the spin-splitter and Edelstein effects, in 3D-stacked versions of the lattice.
method: The study employs the Holstein-Primakoff formalism, Nambu spinor representation, and the Boltzmann transport equation within the relaxation-time approximation, supplemented by Landau-Lifshitz spin-lattice dynamics simulations.
model_or_system: A 2D triangular-lattice Heisenberg antiferromagnet with a 120-degree ground state, extended to a 3D stack of antiferromagnetically coupled layers (an antialtermagnet) under an external magnetic field.
key_observables: Magnon magnetic moment, spin-splitting parameter, spin current (spin-splitter effect), and net magnetization (Edelstein effect).
important_parameters: Exchange constants (J, Jz), magnetic field (B), temperature gradients (grad T), and the low-temperature/small-field limits.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the validity of the harmonic approximation (linearized spin-wave theory), a constant temperature gradient, and the relaxation-time approximation for the Boltzmann equation.
figures_summary: Figures include schematics of the lattice, energy isolines and spin-splitting parameter distribution in the Brillouin zone, Zeeman-splitting of magnon modes, and dispersion relations under different magnetic field strengths.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the 2D triangular-lattice model, analyzes the symmetry and magnetic moment of magnons, extends the model to a 3D stacked antialtermagnet, and concludes by deriving nonlinear thermal transport coefficients and experimental signatures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Triangular lattice of frustrated spins with J > 0. Three uniformly magnetized sublattices m1, m2, and m3 are shown by different colors. The magnetic unit cell is deter- mined by the vectors e1 and e2 and is shown by the orange shading, while a1 and a2 represent the primitive basis vec- tors of the triangular lattice. The magnetic field B is applied perpendicularly to the lattice.

FIG. 2. (a) – The isosurfaces of constant energy εν,k = ε0 are built from the dispersion relations in Eq. (5) for the case B = 0. The value of the magnon magnetic moment in Eq. (6) is shown by the color scheme. The 1st Brillouin zone (1.BZ) corresponding to the magnetic unit cell is shown in gray shad- ing. (b) – Distribution of the spin-splitting parameter λ de- fined in Eq. (7) over the 1.BZ demonstrates the f-wave char- acter of the magnon spectra.

FIG. 3. The effect of the Zeeman splitting of modes ε±1,k0(B) for the fixed wave vector k0 shown by the red dot in Fig. 2(a). Here, lines show the eigen-energies (5) while the magnon in- tensities obtained by means of the spin lattice simulations (see Appendix C for the details) are shown by the color scheme.

FIG. 4. The influence of the applied magnetic field on the magnon modes is shown in terms of the dispersion relations, see panels (a) and (e), and in terms of the reconstructed single spin dynamics, see panels (b-d) and (f-h). The green, red, and blue arrows show three neighboring magnetic moments m1, m2, and m3 belonging to three different sublattices. The ground 120◦state lies in xy-plane. The insets show the dynamics of the vector spin chirality K and the total magnetization m for the cases without (upper row) and with (bottom row) an applied magnetic field, respectively. The applied field is B = 0.1B0 ˆz. For panels (b-d) and (f-h), the wave vector k = 10−3 ˆx/a0 is fixed and close to...

FIG. 5. The influence of the interlayer coupling on the magnon spectra is shown in panels (a,b). The isosurfaces of the constant energy εν,k = 0.6ε0 for all six modes for the case Jz/J = 0.05 are shown in panels (c,d) for different val- ues of kz. The isosurface color shows the magnon magnetic moment. Magnetic field is B = 0.1B0 and B = 0 for panels (a,b), and (c,d), respectively.
